g.data-narration: Segments in Computer Graphics - A fundamental concept for organizing and manipulating graphical elements as logical groups.
A segment is a named collection of display elements like lines, shapes, and colors that can be manipulated as a single unit.
g.text: (primitives + attributes) that can be manipulated as
a single unit. Think of it as a "group" or "layer".

g.data-narration: Segments enable modular design, easy transformation, selective redrawing, and hierarchical modeling for complex graphics.
Every segment follows a lifecycle: CREATE opens a segment, primitives are added, and CLOSE finalizes it.
GKS defines five essential functions: CREATE opens segments, CLOSE finishes them, DELETE removes, RENAME changes names.
Example: CREATE(1) starts segment 1, then primitives are added, and CLOSE() finishes storing the segment.
Four key attributes control segments: Visibility shows/hides, Highlighting emphasizes, Detectability enables picking, and Transformations apply geometric changes.
Important distinction: DELETE removes the segment entirely from memory, while Visibility OFF only hides it temporarily.
The workstation maintains segment storage - a data structure that holds all segments and controls display order.
g.text: Later segments draw on top of earlier ones!

g.data-narration: Hierarchical segments create tree structures where parent segments reference child segments, enabling complex object composition.
Transformations applied to segments affect ALL primitives within them uniformly - translation, rotation, or scaling moves the entire group.
Segments enable interactive graphics: pick a segment, highlight it by changing its attribute, and update the display.
Segments are used in CAD systems for drawing components, animations for moving objects, GUIs for widgets, and interactive graphics.
Key takeaways: Segments group graphics for unified manipulation, follow CREATE-CLOSE lifecycle, and support attributes for control.
g.text: Remove from memory vs hide only

g.data-narration: In summary, segments provide modular, maintainable graphics code - the foundation for modern scene graphs and interactive applications.
